original_abstract:
The effects of pH on nutrient availability are not determined by soil chemistry alone; rather, they emerge from a complex interplay between soil reactions and the biological uptake rates of plants. A critical, yet often overlooked, factor is that both soil particles and plant root surfaces possess variable charges that respond to pH. This shared characteristic frequently influences nutrient availability in opposing directions. 

Sulfate serves as a clear example of this tension: while increasing pH reduces soil sorption—thereby increasing "soil availability"—it simultaneously inhibits the rate of plant uptake, which reduces "plant availability." For phosphate, the inhibitory effect of higher pH on plant uptake is powerful enough to override soil factors, leading to an overall decrease in availability as pH rises. Conversely, for molybdate, the impact of pH on soil adsorption is so dominant that it dictates the net outcome. Cations, such as zinc and copper, exhibit a more balanced dynamic; while soil sorption increases with pH, the rate of plant uptake also increases, resulting in only a modest net decline in availability. Boron remains an outlier because it is primarily absorbed as uncharged boric acid, making its uptake largely independent of surface charge effects. We argue that the traditional scientific focus on soil-centric reactions has created a distorted understanding of how pH actually governs nutrient acquisition in the field.
humanized_abstract:
Whether a pH affects the availability of nutrients in the soil is not a function of soil chemistry alone, but a function of the interplay between the resulting soil chemistry and the plant demand for the nutrient. One frequently overlooked factor is that both soil colloids and plant root surfaces are charged and the charge on these surfaces can vary with pH. The consequences of variable charges on the surfaces of soil colloids and plant roots are frequently opposite in sign and, therefore, opposite in effect on nutrient availability.

Sulfate is just one example in soil chemistry where there is a conflict between increased pH decreasing the soil availability while decreasing the plant availability of the ion. For the case of phosphate the decreased plant availability (which can be quite significant due to severe reduction in plant uptake rate) will often dictate the overall soil availability decreasing with increasing pH. Also, for molybdate the influence of pH on soil adsorption can be so high that this component alone will dictate the overall soil pH-availability trend. The case of cations, such as zinc and copper, lies in between: Soil availability of these cations does indeed increase at increasing pH. However, the increase in plant uptake will not be strongly compromised, as a result that soil pH-availability trend will exhibit only a modest increase towards decreasing availability at increasing pH. Boron, representing one of the exceptions in soil chemistry, has, because of being absorbed by plants predominantly in its neutral uncharged boric acid form, an ion-uptake-availability component which is by and large unaffected by soil surface charge effects. Overall we therefore think that an unbalance in the pH-influences on the soil-plant availability has in general been created due to soil-chemistry orientated general interpretations of the pH-effect on nutrient availability that simply does not correspond to field-conditions of nutrient-plant interactions.

The effects of pH on nutrient availability are not determined by soil chemistry alone; rather, they emerge from a complex interplay between soil reactions and the biological uptake rates of plants.
